title: practical english usage - Part 75
content:
He's very busy. I'm afraid he can't be disturbed.

You were silly not to have locked your car.

notusually                                            or Try not to be late. (                 Try to not be late.  Try to don't be late) Negative infinitives are normally made by putting not before the infinitive.

5 negative       forms:    Try    not    to   be   late.

time) are very unusual.

Progressive perfect passive infinitives (e.g. It must have been being built at the 'What would you like to be doing right now?* 'I'd like to be being massaged.' Progressive passive infinitives are possible but unusual.

They were lucky - they could have been killed.

I'd like to have been sitting there when she walked in.

Perfect progressive and perfect passive infinitives are common.

4 combinations:         to   have      been      sitting;      could      have      been      killed -ing                                 Section8 Infinitives,        forms     and   Past   Participles grammar *91  infinitives without to: I saw you come in.

For verbs that are followed by object + ʃo-infinɪtive, ►    98.

For more information about see, hear, watch, etc + object + verb, ►     110.

For more information about structures with let, ►    512. For make, ►     107.

She was heard to say that she disagreed.

He was made to pay back the money.

In passive versions of these structures the infinitive with to is used.

I've never known him  (to) pay for a drink.

Have Mrs Hansen come in, please, (especially AmE) This structure is also possible with have (►   109) and know (►  504).

Could you help me (to) unload the car?

Help can also be used in this way (►  483).

We both heard him  say that he was leaving. Did you feel the earth move?

/ made them give me the money' back.     I didn 't see you come in.

nn She lets her children staying.. .) She lets her children stay up very late. (wr She lets her children in stay...

infinitive without to. They  include let, make, see, hear, feel, watch and notice.

Certain verbs expressing perception and causation are followed by object + 2 after    let,   make,      hear,     etc She needn't do the washing up.

You'd better see what she wants. I daren't go out at night. (BrE) dare and need (►   431,  532).

We   also use the infinitive without to after had better (►  77), and sometimes after Do you think she might be joking?    I would rather go alone.

1 must  go now. (     I must to go-new.) Can you help me?

and must, we  use the infinitive without to.

After the modal auxiliary verbs will, shall, would, should, can, could, may, might 1  after    modal     auxiliary       verbs without to (sometimes called the ‘ bare infinitive’ ) in some cases.

We   usually use to with infinitives (e.g. I want to know). But we  use the infinitive 91 infinitives       without      to:  I saw     you     come      in.

For more details, see the entries for the different modal verbs.

She could/should/ought to/may/will/must have arrived by now.

unreal, but uncertain.

Modal  verbs with perfect infinitives can also refer to situations that are not She needn't have sent me flowers. (BrE) (She did send flowers.) I would have gone to university if my parents had had more money.

You should have called -1 was getting worried. (The person did not call.) Did you see him  fall? He could have killed himself. (He did not kill himself.) perfect infinitives are also often used to refer to unreal situations.

After the modal verbs could, might, ought, should, would and needn't (BrE), 3 modals:     He  could      have      killed      himself.

/ would have liked to have seen Harry's face.

not change the meaning.

infinitive is sometimes used in informal speech; the extra perfect infinitive does With would like, would prefer and one or two other verbs, a double perfect grammar •  92  infinitive as subject or complement My ambition was to retire at thirty.

Your task is to get across the river without being seen.

An infinitive (clause) can be used after be as a subject complement.

2 complement:        Your      task     is  to   get    across       the    river.
